speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, And we are on tape five of Barbara Traub's interview. I think you were on a four-mile walk to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Didn't know that it was Birkenau, but learned it subsequently. What happened there-- first, we were taken into a huge hall, which was the shower. But prior to that, we were stripped naked, and our hair shorn everywhere-- pubic, everything. I remember the yells. At this point, there were German female guards called Aufseherinnens-- husky, blonde, Brunhildes screaming, yelling, whipping with whip. The whip is in their hand. And I remember the one thing I wasn't going to let go of was my baccalaureate diploma, and it was in a green sort of a book. Stripped naked, shorn, and I was clinging to it just like that. And an Aufseherinnen saw that, and she just ripped it out of my hands, and it flew on the floor. Somebody picked it up, and it was in the garbage pile. And I began to just cry, sob, and it was partly the baccalaureate diploma, because I believe that underneath, without being conscious of that, I felt this was the last stripping away of my identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: If they are taking this away from me as well, I'm alone. I've been separated from my family. They have cut my hair, and I had very long hair in those days. And I remember feeling the hair sort of flowing down my back. I remember it was almost a sensuous feeling as I remembered how the hair, the long hair was cut. What else can they do to me? They are taking away my last contact with someone that I was. And so I began screaming, yelling. I was hit a couple of times, and an elderly woman who was much younger then than I am now-- must've been-- to me, she was an elderly lady, probably in her mid 30s-- came to me and said in Hungarian, child, why are you crying so? I said, my diploma-- they took away my diploma. And she said, forget it, because what you have in your head, that they cannot take away. And you know, that has stayed with me too as one of the human things, humane things, one of the decent things that I remembered, one of the few decent things that I remembered that happened in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, eventually my sobs subsided, and then we were pushed into the shower room, disinfected, sprayed with disinfectant, and taken into another place. And we were given some rags, which were supposed to be dresses, skirts, no underwear, nothing. I got a pair of shoes, two lefties, so it was really-- I mean, for Halloween, you don't get dressed that way. But that's what we had, because we had nothing else. And by this time, it's morning. The smell is there, and now we see not so much flames but smoke. Ah, now we know why the smell. It's a bakery. They were baking bread. Strange smell, but that's what it is. You see the chimneys. Now we see chimneys. Next thing is that I look at someone, and I burst out laughing, because we looked surreal-- shaven, in rags. I mean I remember there was some sort of a window, and a couple of us went to look at ourselves, and we could not recognize ourselves, so we each had to move our heads to know who was who, because it was really not a madhouse. It was bizarre. I mean, you've never seen anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next thing is now we are again whipped in rows, and we are taken somewhere else. And we entered another gate, and there is music. And what is the music? The which is a well-known Hungarian march, marching song. That threw me. I mean, what's going on here? It's really enough to make you lose your sanity. Look what they did to us. I mean, we look like creatures from another world, and the Hungarian what's going on? And who was playing it? An orchestra composed of women, who didn't look as bad as we did, because they had a little hair by now, but they were fiddling away, mostly violinists. That's what I mainly remember. And apparently, I must have had tears in my eyes, because one of them said to me, [HUNGARIAN], you stupid one. Why do you cry? We are receiving you with music. Mm-hm. Well, I know today who they were. They were part of the so-called orchestra, and they were there God knows-- not God knows. I know exactly how many years-- for four years now. And they felt that they felt angry with the Hungarian Jews for two reasons.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, that they let themselves be caught, and secondly, as I've heard it often said during my detention in Auschwitz-Birkenau, while you Hungarians were sleeping under silken comforters, we Polish Jews were laboring here, dying, eaten up by lice. We built this Auschwitz, so there was this duality, [BACKGROUND NOISE] this anger that we were caught, and anger also at we had four good years, while they were in this whole hell. Hell hole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that play itself out during your time in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. The Hungarian-- yes. We got it often-- why didn't you escape? Why did you wait? You idiots, [HUNGARIAN]. Alright. I cannot blame them. They were right. They were right. They suffered, and we had comparative freedom and did nothing with it. The fact that we were ignorant about it is no excuse. Maybe we shouldn't have been ignorant. I don't know. But any rate, next thing was they tattooed us. And I have a tattoo number, and I'm going to show it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] A-7665.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The tattooing? Well, we didn't know why, but, you know, by then, everything that happened so quickly and so horribly-- I can only speak for myself, but I'm sure to some extent, most of us felt the same way. You couldn't absorb one blow before another blow came. So it had a cumulative effect, and you couldn't think. You didn't know what to think, and only thought that I remember having for a long time-- for a couple of weeks, anyway-- was I'll see my mom. I'll see mother. I'll see the children, because I'll see them. I think that was the most important thought, I believe. It was bewildering. You were in a different time warp. You couldn't apply normal thought, or you didn't even have it, because we were barely tattooed. Then we were put into the barracks, and the barracks. I mean, the bed, cots, on the wooden cots on which 15, 16 girls were supposed to sleep like spoons. And if one had to turn, everybody else had to turn, because we were sleeping like this. That you had to get adjusted to, and finally, food. The first time they served food, which arrived at night to Auschwitz-Birkenau, shower, et cetera, tattooing, it must have been noon by then. And it was slop. I mean, I've never seen anything like that. And they put us like about 10 or 15, as many as we were in those bunks. They were called [NON-ENGLISH]. And they gave one dish, and they put that slop, which was allegedly some sort of a soup, and each one was supposed to take so many sips from that one bowl.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just passed it to the next girl, because who was going to do that? And they left it there. The first day, the second day, the third day, it was different. How was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The third day, we began eating the slop, because we were beginning to starve, because there was nothing else. They kept us for about four weeks. You see, here, I don't have the exact time element, because you forget dates. You forget-- it's not that you forget. You don't know anymore whether it's the 15th or the 18th. You just know it's day, and it's night. It's another day. It's another night. And after about four weeks, I believe, we were quarantined, which meant we were kept in that particular block. And then we learned what [NON-ENGLISH] appell, meaning roll call, was. And they kept counting us three times a day-- early in the morning, midday, and in the evening or at sundown. When I say early in the morning, I mean at dawn when the rouse got us out from the barracks, and then noon, and-- and-- and sundown time. And there was very little food, and they began-- the rows, we were always in rows of five-- the rows began to thin, because after the person in charge of that particular block where I was, and she was called the blokova-- Polish words-- she set us up. She organized us in rows of five. She began to count each time with her numbered what she was supposed to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she kept counting and counting and made mistakes, and somebody wasn't there, and then this went on and on and on for hours sometimes. Then came the [GERMAN] to count. Again, she too didn't have the exact numbers, or somebody just fell down, passed out-- again, counting. And the final count was done by the SS. The SS counted, but at the same time, weeded, selected. And so the numbers we didn't still know why, although by this time, two weeks later, the old timers, as we used to call them, whom we constantly kept asking, tell us. When are we going to meet our mothers, our children, et cetera? They would say, see? That's where they went. At first, I remember clearly saying to myself, this poor woman lost her mind. I mean she's crazy. I mean everybody knows it's a bakery. It has a funny smell, but it's a bakery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somehow two, three weeks later, we know it was not a bakery. And even though we didn't talk about it, even I didn't talk about it to anyone, I knew the second big lie [BACKGROUND NOISE] when the SS so politely said to me, [GERMAN]. But Miss, we are civilized people. You are going to see your mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with? Were you with Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was a Hungarian-- you see, after four or six weeks of quarantine--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Why were you quarantined?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe in Lager A, which is part of Birkenau. Well, Birkenau is part of Auschwitz. They had Lager A, B, C. B was the work Lager. C was the worst, and at the time I got to C, I called this Anus Mundi, which you know probably what it means.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: How should I translate it from Latin into a more acceptable term? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I'll think about it. It means really it's the worst hole there is, which is an anal hole, all right. It's the hole of the anus, Anus Mundi. Actually, Anus Mundi, it is the world's anus. That's the exact translation. So that was Lager C. The quarantine was in Lager A, and after a number of weeks, those who made it during this time survived it, because one of the terrible things that was happening was diarrhea, which killed many of the young women. Why diarrhea? Because everybody was so terribly thirsty, and the water was polluted, and they drank water, and diarrhea took over. And then eventually, they were selected during [NON-ENGLISH] appells. I was particularly lucky, I would say, because for some reason, my thirst stopped. And I didn't need to drink water, and I became constipated, as a matter of fact. Until this day, when people tell me I should drink x number of glasses of water a day, I say to them, but you don't understand. My lack of desire for water saved my life. And so I was put at first in a work commando in Lager B, into another block, into another quarter. And I did all kind of work at first-- field work, carrying stones from one place to another, digging ditches, and so forth. Then I was selected one day to be part of a commando called The Brzezinka Commando, because the women were wearing white scarves, and I was put in the night working group to open those valises that we left on the train. And that I did for about two months. By this time, before I was part of this Brzezinka commando, hunger was endemic. By this time, and I'm speaking for myself, hunger was the most powerful force that kept me alive, because hunger made me scrounge for a potato peel, if you will. Hunger also obliterated any insight into what was happening. I couldn't think of anything else but hunger. And maybe that was why when I finally knew that that poor old timer was telling me the truth when she was pointing to the chimneys, I was hungry. And it was like an earthquake, but the aftershock of the earthquake that came much later was the more terrible, precisely because there was no morning, when morning should have been the proper time for it. And while I was in this Brzezinka, I found-- I find-- I found food in some of the valises, and I used to smuggle them in. For instance, toothpaste was a great contraband, because there was black market. You would exchange a diamond for a piece of bread. And I would line my sleeves with toothpaste, and give it to some of the girls with whom I shared the bunk. And it was very painful, because I opened valises, and I found children's clothing, and I found pictures, and I found the whole background of thousands and thousands of Hungarian Jews, because we were the first transport, as I learned much later. My town and my transport was the first one.

speaker: interviewer
text: After about four or eight weeks, I was put in another commando called the [NON-ENGLISH] which was a factory on the premises somewhere in Lager B. We walked. How did we walk? With the SS, with the dog, with the truncheons hitting, and we walked-- there was a big road separating the men's [INAUDIBLE]-- the man's camps from the women's camp. And the men would come, and they would see women go through the road. They would come close to the wire-- not too close, because the wire, as you know, were electrified-- and would yell out names. And the women would yell back, yes, she's here. And then we would ask for names, and whoever was caught, the SS would hit.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was done anyway. And I kept asking for my father. I kept asking for my father, because I thought that he made it. I kept asking for my brother, my older brother, because my Alex I knew was somewhere in Hungary. I never got any information. I was in the [NON-ENGLISH] until about November. But what I remember, and I would like to tell that story of Yom Kippur in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was Yom Kippur in Auschwitz, because word got around. Somebody was keeping some count, and word got around. And at night in that particular block, as I'm sure in all the other blocks, women were singing the And next morning, we went out for [NON-ENGLISH] appell. They brought coffee. Nobody touched it. There was no communication, but it just so happened they took us to the [NON-ENGLISH]. When I say they, I mean the SS, and we did the required quota, or we didn't make the required quota, because it was impossible anyway. And this hitting and the slapping and whatnot-- came noon, and now we stand in a row, single rowed, and strangely, this time, the two barrels of what used to be just slop is the distribution of this slop into huge barrels, is being supervised by this SS. I don't know how it happened, but I happen to be first in line, and I had by that time somehow organized, gotten around to have my own little tin cup. And I stand there, and he says, come-- come. Somebody pushes me. I go. And he lifts, and as I went a couple of steps, somehow instinctively, I put both my hands behind my back, including the tin cup, at which point the SS dips the ladle into the barrel of soup, lifts it, and lets it drip back-- the soup, the liquid-- saying to me, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. This is Yom Kippur. But I'm not thinking of Yom Kippur really in those terms. Yes, as he did that, I was very, very hungry, but I sort of remembered the night before the chant, the And I say, nein. He does it again, and I still keep my hands back. And then he takes the ladle with the soup and splashes it in my face and calls me [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the usual terms that the Nazis used for us-- dirty Jew, pig Jew, whatnot. And now the soup, the liquid drips down my face, and I have this urge to at least lick it, because it's full of potatoes. I mean he was showing it to me. That's what he was showing to me that it wasn't a slop this time. It was full of chunks of potatoes, chunks of meat, and here, this thing is dripping down my face, and I so want to lick it. But I didn't. I didn't. And then he sort of said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], called me a name. Came the other girl, and she said, nein. Nobody took it. After I think the 10th or 15th girl, he kicked the barrel of soup, and it seeped. Nobody touched it. That's the one moment in my entire Auschwitz incarceration and elsewhere that I really-- I didn't think it through, but something made me say no, because it was Yom Kippur, and he was trying to defile me even more with-- with food, which was life, in a sense. And I said no, and we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you have the strength?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I really don't know. I really don't know. It was-- I don't know whether I would have done it if the night before there wouldn't have been some sort of a Nidrei. I mean that really evoked--

speaker: interviewer
text: I was going to ask you about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: --some very strong feelings of-- of-- of memories of belonging somewhere, of being part of something, of having a legacy, a heritage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the night before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, word must have gotten around that it was the [INAUDIBLE] Yom Kippur. And we did have that piece of bread, which was given at sundown, which was anyway OK. It was sundown. And then just one of the girls, somebody who-- who had probably a good voice, but more importantly, knew the melody and knew the words. And just it was so quiet that when she sung, normally there were yells and screams and pushing and cryings and bickering among the inmates. We were miserable. We were terrible. We were hungry. We were frightened. We were so lonely, so alone, so abandoned. We didn't know what was going on. And this time, you could hear a sigh, you could hear it when this woman sang the

speaker: interviewer
text: So it must have been that that made me say no. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I did not think it through. I did not, because he could have beaten me to death. But for once, I felt I can refuse something which goes against my grain. That was the only moment that I think back while I was in Auschwitz that I did what I'm glad I did. It's not that I did anything bad or anything good. I was just a cog in the wheel. But I did something beyond what was expected of me. By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By everybody else, certainly by the SS, certainly by the Amalek, certainly by the evil person, certainly by he who wanted to destroy me, first destroy me, humiliate me, take away my identity, make me into a nothing. And food-- food, the most exciting, the most potent thing in Auschwitz, because food was life in a way. Food was power, but in that case, that Yom Kippur, it was not morality. I mean, that's how I understand it today. But I assure you then, I couldn't think. I just knew I wanted to lick-- at least to have the taste of that thick soup, and I wasn't going to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change tapes.
